aliases.name: Ahp
aliases.meaning: from Sanskrit āpyati, 'to cause to suffer'

description.summary: A female nocturnal spirit of mainland Southeast Asian folklore, manifesting as a floating disembodied woman's head from which the heart, stomach and other internal organs trail. Belief in the Krasue remains active in rural Thailand, Laos and Cambodia.
description.full: The Krasue (Thai: กระสือ; Khmer: អាប, Ahp; Lao: Kasu) is a female spirit of mainland Southeast Asian folklore. By day she lives as an ordinary woman; at night her head detaches from her body and floats free, the heart, stomach and a length of intestine still attached and dangling beneath. In this form she hunts for blood and raw flesh, often preying on livestock or on pregnant women and newborns, and is associated with luminous lights seen drifting across rice fields. The headless body must be hidden during the night and the head must rejoin it before sunrise; if the body is destroyed or hidden too well, the Krasue will die. Belief in the Krasue remains widespread in rural Thailand, Laos and Cambodia. The figure belongs to a wider Southeast Asian complex of detached-head female spirits that includes the Khmer Ahp, Lao Kasu, Malay Penanggal, Indonesian Kuyang and Balinese Leyak, and Filipino Manananggal.

etymology.origin: Thai กระสือ (krasue). The Khmer cognate Ahp is traditionally derived from Sanskrit आप्यति (āpyati), meaning 'to cause anyone to suffer'.
etymology.meaning: The vernacular Thai name has no fixed lexical meaning beyond identifying the spirit; in usage 'krasue' is now generic for this category of detached-head ghost.
etymology.first_use: Documented in Thai and Khmer folklore long before modern attestation; first systematic English-language description in late 19th- and early 20th-century colonial ethnographies of Southeast Asia.

related_phenomena.name: Leyak
related_phenomena.description: Balinese practitioner of black magic with a detached flying head

related_phenomena.name: Manananggal
related_phenomena.description: Philippine winged vampire that severs at the waist

related_phenomena.name: Langsuir
related_phenomena.description: Malay female revenant of women who died in childbirth

related_phenomena.name: Rokurokubi
related_phenomena.description: Japanese yōkai whose neck stretches at night

coloration: luminous greenish or reddish glow around organs

features.description: Head of a woman, often described as young and beautiful, separated from the body and floating above the ground
Heart, stomach and a length of intestine hang from the neck; some accounts add lungs and liver
The head and entrails are described as glowing, often reddish or greenish, and are associated with strange lights seen at night in fields
Modern depictions add pointed fangs and a long tongue used for feeding

features.feature: Day form indistinguishable from a normal woman
features.description: By daylight the cursed individual appears completely human and lives in the community, sometimes unaware of her own condition

physical.morphology: The Krasue is a hybrid 'living-ghost' rather than a true revenant: a living woman whose head and viscera detach at night and reattach at dawn. The torso remains inert and hidden during the nocturnal flight.

abilities.description: The head and dangling organs hover and move through the air at night
Drinks blood (notably from livestock such as water buffalo and chickens) and consumes raw flesh, carrion, placenta and excrement

abilities.ability: Predation on pregnant women and newborns
abilities.description: Particularly associated with attacks on pregnant women and infants; placenta is a sought-after food

Cambodian Ahp tradition holds that the curse must be passed to another woman (often a daughter or relative, sometimes via shared food or saliva) for the carrier to escape future rebirth as the same spirit

behavior.diet: Blood, raw flesh, carrion, placenta, feces; livestock blood (water buffalo, chickens) when human prey is unavailable
patterns: Active only between dusk and dawn
Must rejoin headless body before sunrise or die
Vulnerable to thorns and spikes that snag the trailing entrails
Drawn to pregnant women, newborns and fresh corpses
Associated with strange floating lights in rice fields

range.description: Belief in the Krasue and its cognates spans mainland Southeast Asia and the Malay Archipelago, with the strongest contemporary tradition in rural Thailand, Laos and Cambodia. Closely related figures are reported throughout Malaysia, Indonesia (including Bali), the Philippines and parts of Vietnam.

hotspots.name: Isan (northeastern Thailand)

location.description: Rural Isan provinces, where Krasue belief and reported sightings remain strong

hotspots.notes: Rural Isan is the heartland of contemporary Krasue belief, with periodic local panics over reported sightings.

hotspots.name: Cambodia (Ahp tradition)
hotspots.notes: Cambodian Ahp belief frames the spirit as a hereditary curse that must be passed on; protective practices around childbirth (notably regarding the placenta) are widespread.

first_recorded.source: Mainland Southeast Asian oral tradition; described in 19th- and early-20th-century colonial-era ethnographic literature on the region.

peak_activity_periods.description: Embedded in folk Buddhist and animist belief throughout mainland Southeast Asia.
Sustained presence as a horror-film and television icon, especially in Thai and Cambodian cinema, while remaining a live folk belief in rural communities.

temporal.seasonal_patterns: Reported sightings cluster around hot dry-season nights when strange lights are visible across paddy fields.

origins.cultural_roots: Rooted in the animist substrate of mainland Southeast Asian religion, layered with Theravada Buddhist concepts of karmic punishment. The Krasue is widely understood as a person cursed for a serious moral or magical transgression in this or a previous life.
origins.earliest_accounts: Oral tradition predating reliable written record; described in 19th- and early-20th-century European colonial ethnographies of Southeast Asia, and in vernacular Thai, Khmer and Lao literature.
origins.folklore_connections: Part of a broader Southeast Asian complex of female blood-drinking spirits — Penanggal (Malay), Leyak (Balinese), Manananggal (Filipino), Langsuir (Malay) — sharing the motif of a woman whose body or head separates at night to feed.

timeline.event: Krasue/Ahp/Kasu belief established across mainland Southeast Asia as part of folk religion
Thai film 'Krasue Sao' released, establishing Krasue as a mainstay of Thai horror cinema
timeline.significance: Beginning of the modern Thai-cinematic Krasue

timeline.event: Thai film 'Demonic Beauty' (Krasue) released, treating the Krasue legend through a romantic-horror frame
timeline.significance: Mainstream regional revival of the figure

timeline.event: CCTV footage from a lemon-juice factory in Pathum Thani Province, Thailand, was widely reported as depicting a Krasue
timeline.significance: High-profile modern 'sighting' that received national news coverage; an imaging analyst suggested an explanation involving light reflection

timeline.event: Hundreds of teenagers in Ayutthaya Province, Thailand, gathered to watch red lights moving over rice fields, attributed locally to a Krasue
timeline.significance: Crowd-scale modern Krasue panic; landowner attributed the lights to bird-scaring lanterns

timeline.event: Thai romantic-horror film 'Inhuman Kiss' (Sang Krasue) released, gaining international attention as Thailand's submission for the Academy Awards Best International Feature consideration
timeline.significance: Most internationally visible recent Krasue production

history.modern_era: Krasue belief remains widespread in rural Thailand, Laos and Cambodia, with periodic local panics over strange lights or accusations of Krasue identity. The figure is a recurring icon of Thai horror cinema and television, and has crossed over into pan-Asian and Western horror media (e.g. anthology episodes on streaming services and inclusion as a playable character in the 2025 update of the video game 'Dead by Daylight').

notable.name: Pathum Thani factory CCTV footage

location.description: Lemon-juice factory in Pathum Thani Province

notable.description: Closed-circuit camera footage from a lemon-juice factory in Pathum Thani Province appeared to show a luminous floating object resembling a Krasue. The clip received heavy national coverage. A visual-imaging specialist consulted by Thai media suggested the appearance was best explained by light reflection rather than a paranormal entity.

credibility.notes: Most-cited modern Krasue 'sighting' in Thai media.

notable.name: Ayutthaya rice-field lights

credibility_factors: Large crowd of witnesses to ambient light phenomenon

notable.description: Hundreds of teenagers gathered to watch unexplained red lights moving over rice fields in Ayutthaya Province. The phenomenon was widely attributed to a Krasue. The owner of the land said the lights were floating lanterns used to scare birds away from the crop.

credibility.notes: Illustrative of how unexplained lights are folded into the active Krasue belief system.

photographic.name: Pathum Thani factory CCTV still
photographic.description: Frame from Thai factory CCTV footage interpreted as a Krasue.
photographic.analysis: Visual-imaging specialist consulted by Thai media argued the footage was consistent with a light-reflection artefact.

paranormal.name: Cursed-woman tradition
paranormal.description: The Krasue is a real woman under a karmic or magical curse — typically for sins committed in a past life, for the practice of black magic that went wrong, or as inherited from a maternal lineage — whose head and viscera detach at night.
evidence_cited: Continuity of belief across mainland Southeast Asia

scientific.name: Methane / wetland luminescence hypothesis
scientific.description: Strange lights reported as Krasue sightings are produced by ignition of methane and other gases released from decomposing organic matter in tropical wetlands and rice fields.
evidence_cited: Known production of swamp-gas luminescence in wet tropical environments
Reports of Krasue lights specifically in paddy fields

skeptical.name: Critique of the methane explanation
skeptical.description: Associate Professor Dr. Sirintornthep Towprayoon of King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi has publicly disputed the methane hypothesis, arguing that methane production over Thai farmland is insufficient for self-ignition and that surface combustion would not produce a freely floating, mobile light.
evidence_cited: Methane-emission measurements in Thai paddy ecosystems

skeptical.name: Cultural transmission and misidentification
skeptical.description: Modern Krasue 'sightings' are explained by a combination of culturally primed misperception, mundane lights (lanterns, vehicle reflections, fireflies, swamp luminescence) and CCTV/camera artefacts in a population in which the figure remains a live cultural reference.
evidence_cited: Bird-scaring lanterns identified in the 2014 Ayutthaya panic
Light-reflection analysis offered for the 2008 Pathum Thani CCTV clip

traditions: Thai folk Buddhist tradition of the krasue as karmic punishment
Cambodian Ahp tradition of inherited curse and required curse-transmission
stories: Folk story of a Khmer princess cursed into Krasue form (popularised by Thai cinema)
Village accounts of women accused of being Krasue in rural Thailand and Cambodia
folklore.beliefs: The Krasue is widely understood as a curse on a living woman rather than a free-floating ghost. Protective practices include surrounding sleeping people with thorny branches that the entrails cannot pass; hiding or destroying the headless body during the night; in Cambodia, burying the placenta of a newborn far from the home so an Ahp cannot reach it.

film_tv: Witch with the Flying Head (Hong Kong, 1977)
other: Recurring icon of Thai horror tourism and ghost-story media
Used colloquially in Thai expressions: 'eating like a Krasue' (gluttony)

research.ongoing_studies: No formal scientific research programme exists; the Krasue is studied chiefly by anthropologists and folklorists working on Southeast Asian belief systems.
